content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
, (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Juan F. Garza, Councilmember 
City of Bellflower 
16600 Civic Center Drive 
Bellflower, CA 90706 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-187 
Dear Mr. Garza: 
August 25, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the .. Act").1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section I 090. 
QUESTION 
As a City of Bellflower Councilmember, will you have a disqualifying conflict of interest in 
making governmental decisions regarding the requirement, application, and permitting processes 
for cannabis businesses when your employer represents clients in the cannabis industry? 
CONCLUSION 
No. Any financial effect on your employer through the City Council's decisions regarding 
the requirement, application, and permitting processes for cannabis-businesses is not material. 
FACTS 
You are a City of Bellflower (44City") councilmember. You are also employed by the 24-
person consulting firm, Englander Knabe and Allen ('"firm"). According to the firm's website, it 
serves several industries. Namely, the firm serves the aviation; communication technology; energy; 
financial services; health care; law firms; municipal government; public safety; restaurants and 
hospitality; transportation and infrastructure, and; waste management industries. 
I 
, 
i The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-17-187 
Page No. 2 
The finn recently began representing clients in the cannabis industry as well. You infonned 
me that the finn devotes approximately five percent of its consulting services to serving clients in 
the cannabis industry. The represented cannabis businesses operate in approximately three cities 
other than Bellflower- Los Angeles, Lancaster, and Desert Hot Springs. You personally do not 
directly represent or consult with any clients in the cannabis industry. Furthennore, you do not 
receive income from cannabis-related clients nor have you referred a cannabis-related client to the 
firm. 
The City Council is establishing the requirements for cannabis-related businesses, including 
the zoning restrictions that will dictate where the businesses will be allowed to operate in the City. 
The City Council will also address various policy matters relative to cannabis operations within the 
City, including the following: the total number of operational pennits that may be used; distance 
requirements for dispensaries from religious institutions; whether medical cannabis dispensaries 
should be allowed before recreational cannabis; the types of property interests that must be owned 
by applicants; whether operation permit applications must be submitted within a certain timeframe 
for consideration or whether the City may accept them on a rolling basis; whether the City Council 
should have the ability to authorize the transfer of operation permits, and; the amount of the 
proposed application fees for processing cannabis-related conditional use permits and operations 
permits. 
Once requirements are established, there will be an application process, followed by a 
permitting process in which the City Council will decide which business will be approved to hold a 
pennit in the City. There are not any particular cannabis businesses known or being discussed for 
approval currently. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
(Section 87103.) Section 82048 defines "public official" as every member, officer, employee, or 
consultant of a state or local government agency, including city councilmembers. Interests from 
which a conflict of interest may arise are defined in Section 87103 and include the following: 
. 
. 
• 
Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the 
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, 
aggregating $500 or more in value provided to, received by, or promised to, the public 
official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. 
According to your facts, you have a financial interest in the firm as a source of income. 
(Section 87103(c).) Section 87103 also indicates that an official has a financial interest in his or her 
pertonal finances, including those of "his or her immediate family," also known as the "personal 
financial effects" rule.2 
2 Effects on an official's personal finances are not considered separately from the effect on an official's interest 
in a business entity. (Regulation 18702.S(c).) Thus, we do not consider the personal financial effects rule further. 


Foreseeability: 
File No. A-17-187 
Page No. 3 
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a 
governmental decision on a public official's interest is material. (Section 87100.) The standard for 
foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. 
(Regulation 18701.) An interest is explicitly involved in a decision if the interest is a named party 
in, or the subject of, the governmental decision. (Regulation 18701 (a).) Under the facts you have 
provided, the firm is not explicitly involved in the decisions. Thus, Regulation 18701 (b) provides: 
"[i]n general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than 
hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable."3 
Materiality - Business Entity as Source of Income: 
Regulation 18702.1 provides the standard for materiality when the income is for services or 
a salary and the source is a business entity. (Regulation 18702.3(a)(4).) Under Regulation 
18702.1 (b ), an effect is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it 
reasonably foreseeable that the effect would contribute to a change in the value of the business 
entity. The regulation also provides examples of decisions that may have a material effect on a 
business entity, including a decision that would authorize, prohibit, regulate, or otherwise establish 
conditions for an activity in which the business entity is engaged. (Regulation 18702.1 (b )(I). 
Under the facts that you have provided, the firm only derives a small portion of their income 
from consulting services devoted to cannabis businesses. You do not personally represent any of 
those businesses. Moreover, the City Council is unaware of the identity of those businesses during 
the requirements process. Even if the cannabis business clients overlap with those seeking 
permitting in the City of Bellflower, it is unlikely that such a small increase in the firm's revenue 
would materially affect its value if you were to generally vote in favor of expansion of the cannabis 
industry in Bellflower. Accordingly, the financial effect of the City Council's decisions that may 
affect your financial interest are not material. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
RPOC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Jack C. Woodside 
General Counsel 
 [.Q) 
By: 
Ryan P. O'Connor 
Counsel, Legal Di vision 
3 Regulation 1870 I (b) also provides a non-exhaustive list of six factors that may be considered in the 
"reasonably foreseeable" detennination.

for cannabis businesses when your employer represents clients in the cannabis industry?

the requirement, application, and permitting processes for cannabis-businesses is not material.

QUESTION: As a City of Bellflower Councilmember, will you have a disqualifying conflict of interest in 
making governmental decisions regarding the requirement, application, and permitting processes 
for cannabis businesses when your employer represents clients in the cannabis industry?

CONCLUSION: No. Any financial effect on your employer through the City Council's decisions regarding 
the requirement, application, and permitting processes for cannabis-businesses is not material.
